_note: Descriptions and examples are reproduced from the official PMD documentation at pmd.github.io. This is not a hand-crafted file — content is sourced directly from PMD's published rule pages.

rules.description: References to `System.out`, `System.err`, `System.in`, and `System.console()` are generally used for debugging purposes and can remain in the codebase unintentionally. This rule detects calls to `System.out.println`, `System.err.println` and similar methods.
Avoid `printStackTrace()`; use a logger call instead. Stack trace printing obscures the exception context and is not configurable or filterable in production logging systems.
Avoid using implementation types like `ArrayList`, `LinkedList`, `HashMap` and `HashSet` as object references. Use the respective interface types instead, such as `List`, `Map`, `Set` and `Queue`.
Use `isEmpty()` to verify if the collection is empty instead of comparing the size to 0. The `isEmpty()` method is more readable and expressive of intent.
Calls to `System.exit()`, `Runtime.exit()`, and `Runtime.halt()` terminate the JVM and should be avoided when possible. Use exceptions to propagate errors instead.
Use `ArrayList` instead of `Vector`. `Vector` is obsolete and synchronized by default, incurring unnecessary overhead when synchronization is not needed.
Throwing a new exception from a catch block without wrapping the original exception loses the original stack trace. Use the original exception as the cause: `throw new RuntimeException("msg", e)`.
Detects when a private field is declared and/or assigned a value but is never used. Unused private fields are dead code and should be removed.
Empty catch blocks prevent exceptions from being handled, causing bugs to fail silently. Log the exception or rethrow it so failures are visible.
The use of string concatenation inside a loop is wasteful and inefficient. Use a `StringBuffer` or `StringBuilder` instead.
Each caught exception type should be handled in its own catch clause instead of using an `instanceof` check in a single catch block. Catch-by-type is more readable and idiomatic.
Identifies private fields whose values never change once they are initialized and which could be made `final`. Making immutable fields `final` documents intent and prevents accidental reassignment.
This rule detects when a class defines a constructor that is not required. The default constructor performs the same function as a generated constructor. Unnecessary constructors are noise.

Thread safety: calling `notify()` on a shared reference can cause starvation because it wakes only one waiting thread, chosen arbitrarily. Use `notifyAll()` to wake all waiters and avoid the starvation risk.
